name: Urwa ibn Mas'ud
epithet: The Chief Who Came Home to His People
title: Urwa ibn Mas'ud (RA): The Chief Who Came Home to His People
meta_description: The story of Urwa ibn Mas'ud (RA), the noble chief of Ta'if who resembled Isa (AS), negotiated at Hudaybiyyah, and returned to call his own people to Islam.
keywords: Urwa ibn Mas'ud, story of Urwa, chief of Ta'if, Banu Thaqif, Hudaybiyyah, resembled Isa, sahaba stories, The Firsts
lede: He had standing in two cities, the trust of warring tribes, and a face that an authentic narration compares to Isa, the son of Mary. By every measure his people respected, he had already won.

This is the story of Urwa ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him): the most noble man of Ta'if, who watched the love that surrounded the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, came back to embrace Islam, and then went home to a people he was sure would never harm him.

sections.h2: A man honoured in two cities
paras: Urwa ibn Mas'ud belonged to Banu Thaqif, the leading tribe of Ta'if. In that world a person was known by tribe more than by city, and his lineage joined the best of both towns. His father, Mas'ud, was counted among the most noble men of Ta'if. His mother came from the nobles of Quraysh in Makkah, which gave Urwa credibility and standing in each place at once.

Ta'if sits about seventy miles from Makkah. The two cities were tied together by marriage, by trade, and by a shared sense of who counted as noble. A man honoured in one was honoured in the other, and Urwa was honoured in both.

His mother was remembered not only for her standing but for her intelligence. Urwa could recall, from when he was a small child, a battle in which the tribes of Arabia were killing one another. She saw the danger coming, pitched a tent, and told her sons to offer it as refuge to anyone fleeing the fighting. She tended the wounded and brought them water, and those who escaped death that day never forgot the family that had sheltered them.

Urwa carried that instinct into his own life. To stop a cycle of revenge between tribes, he once paid the blood money owed for old killings out of his own pocket, gathering the wealthy of the community to compensate the injured and restore calm to Makkah. Some accounts say he was still paying off that debt at the end of his life.

This was the reputation he built in the years before Islam: a merchant who travelled widely, a man who liked peace, who brought tribes together rather than setting them against each other. He did not pit Ta'if against Makkah. He looked for the way back to calm.

When the people of Makkah turned on the Prophet ﷺ, many of them abandoned the very code of honour they had once been proud of. They lied, they schemed, they broke their own customs to shut down his message. Urwa was not a man you found doing any of that.

That steadiness is part of why his story matters. He was not won over by panic or pressure, and he was not the kind to play tricks. When his heart finally turned, it turned for reasons he had weighed carefully, which is perhaps why it held all the way to the end.

Urwa had one of the most distinctive descriptions among the companions. In an authentic narration, the Prophet ﷺ described the prophets he was shown on the Night Journey, and he said that the person who most resembled Isa, the son of Mary, was Urwa ibn Mas'ud. He was a man of striking and dignified appearance: long hair, a strong build, a settled place among his people.

It is a quiet detail with a lot in it. The Prophet ﷺ was naming a likeness to a prophet of God, given to a man who, at the time, had not accepted the message at all. Honour was honour, and the Prophet ﷺ saw it where it sat.

Urwa is also mentioned, in his way, in the Qur'an. When the message first reached the people, they objected that revelation should have come to one of the great men of the two cities, not to an orphan of modest means. Urwa was one of the men they had in mind.

ayah.text: and they said, 'Why was this Quran not sent down to a distinguished man, from either of the two cities?'

paras: For years Ta'if mostly stayed out of the conflict. Urwa enters the story at Hudaybiyyah, when the Prophet ﷺ came from Madinah in peace, unarmed, intending only to make pilgrimage. Makkah was caught: to fight him would violate their own sacred customs, to let him through would feel like defeat. So they sent negotiators, and Urwa came as one of them. He had the perfect standing for it. He knew Makkah without belonging to the tribe that carried the worst of the bad blood, and he was trusted enough to be heard.

He tried a hard line first. He looked at the mixed gathering around the Prophet ﷺ, people of every tribe and class, and warned that such a crowd would scatter and abandon him at the first sign of danger. The companions held their composure, but the words stung. When one of them answered him sharply, Urwa was offended, until he learned it was Abu Bakr who had spoken. Then he let it go, because he remembered that Abu Bakr had once done him a favour, and he was not a man to forget a kindness.

There was one more interruption. As Urwa spoke, he reached toward the Prophet ﷺ in the familiar way of his people, and an armoured companion moved his hand back with the flat of a sword and told him to keep it away. Urwa asked who this rough man was, and learned it was his own nephew. He was a chief, unused to being checked like that, and slowly it was dawning on him that this was not a gathering he could push around.

Then he watched more closely. He saw companions who would not raise their voices in front of the Prophet ﷺ, who almost competed to serve him, who would not give him up for anything. Urwa had stood in the courts of the most powerful kings of his age, in the palaces of Caesar, of Kisra, and of the Negus. He went back to Makkah and told them plainly: he had never seen a people love a leader the way these people loved this man, and they would never abandon him.

Makkah did not listen. The treaty came, the treaty was broken, and in time the Prophet ﷺ entered Makkah not with revenge but with mercy, forgiving the people who had driven him out. When the news of that mercy spread, something settled in Urwa's heart.

He had been away learning the tools of war when Ta'if was besieged, so he had missed those events. But when he heard how the Prophet ﷺ had pardoned the very people who had oppressed him for years, Urwa travelled to Madinah of his own choosing and embraced Islam. The Prophet ﷺ received him with open joy, and the same companions who had once traded harsh words with him now competed to host him in their homes.

Then Urwa asked to go back to Ta'if and call his people to faith. The Prophet ﷺ warned him gently that they would kill him, that they were not ready. Urwa could not believe it. My people love me so much, he said, that if they found me asleep they would not dare to wake me. The Prophet ﷺ, who had once been loved by his people and then run out of his home, knew exactly how that hope could end. But Urwa insisted, sure he would bring them back as Muslims.

It is worth pausing on how far he had come. The man who arrived at Hudaybiyyah to push the Muslims around was now begging for the chance to risk his life for them. Nothing had forced his hand. No army stood over Ta'if. He had simply seen the truth, and once he saw it, he wanted his own people to have it too.

Urwa returned to Ta'if after a long absence. When his people greeted him in the old way, he answered with the greeting of the people of Paradise, salam, and told them he had become a Muslim and wished the same for them. That night there were angry words in his house, a struggle, hands raised, but no real harm, and they left assuming the matter would cool by morning.

At dawn Urwa stood on his rooftop and gave the call to prayer, the first adhan ever raised in Ta'if. His own people answered it with arrows. He was struck and did not die at once. When his kin spoke of killing the leaders responsible, he refused. Do not kill anyone for me, he said. Let my blood be a charity. Let there be peace between you. He asked only to be buried with the martyrs who had fallen near Ta'if before the army left.

When the news reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ likened Urwa to the believing man of Surah Ya-Sin: the man who came to his people calling them to the truth, and whom they killed for it. Of that man the Qur'an records the words he spoke once he had crossed over, and they fit Urwa exactly.

ayah.text: He was told, 'Enter the Garden,' so he said, 'If only my people knew how my Lord has forgiven me and set me among the highly honoured.'

lessons_heading: What the life of Urwa teaches us
lessons_intro: Urwa had everything his society called success, and he gave it to a truth that cost him his life. His example is quiet, but it is hard to forget.

lessons.bold: Remember the good others have done.
lessons.text: Urwa swallowed an insult because he could not forget an old kindness. Loyalty that does not erase the favours done for it is a noble trait, and Islam came to keep traits like it, not undo them.

lessons.bold: Notice what real devotion looks like.
lessons.text: He had seen the greatest kings on earth, yet it was the love around an unarmed man that changed him. Sometimes the truest sign of something true is the way it is loved.

lessons.bold: Carry the message home, even when it is hardest.
lessons.text: Urwa went straight to the people most likely to reject him, because they were his own. Faith often asks the most of us in our own homes and towns.

lessons.bold: Let go of the right to revenge.
lessons.text: Struck down by his own people, he forbade any retaliation and asked that his blood be a charity. He wanted peace for them more than he wanted justice for himself.

faq.a: He was the most noble man of Banu Thaqif, the leading tribe of Ta'if, and a respected peacemaker known in both Ta'if and Makkah. He embraced Islam after the conquest of Makkah and died a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

faq.q: Why is Urwa said to have resembled Isa?
faq.a: In an authentic narration about the Night Journey, the Prophet ﷺ said that among all people the one who most resembled Isa, the son of Mary, was Urwa ibn Mas'ud. He was a man of dignified and striking appearance.

faq.q: What role did Urwa play at Hudaybiyyah?
faq.a: He came as one of the negotiators sent by Makkah. While there he observed the deep love and discipline of the companions toward the Prophet ﷺ, and he returned to warn Makkah that these people would never abandon him.

faq.q: How did Urwa ibn Mas'ud die?
faq.a: After embracing Islam, he returned to Ta'if to call his people to faith. When he gave the call to prayer from his rooftop, his own people struck him with arrows. He forbade any revenge and asked to be buried with the martyrs of Ta'if.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Urwa had every reason to stay comfortable. He was respected, settled, and safe. Instead he gave his standing and finally his life to something he had only recently come to believe, and as he died he asked that no one be harmed in his name. That is a rare kind of courage, the kind that thinks of others even at the end.

Most of us will not face arrows. But we all face the harder rooms: the family that knew us before we changed, the people who expect us to stay exactly as we were. His life is an invitation to carry what is true into those rooms anyway, gently, and to hold no grudge when it is not received. Bring that into one conversation today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Urwa ibn Mas'ud (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Qur'an translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 43:31 and 36:26-27). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

social.caption:
Urwa ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most noble man of Ta'if: a peacemaker trusted in two cities, and the companion an authentic narration says most resembled Isa, the son of Mary.

He first met the Prophet ﷺ as an opponent, sent to negotiate at Hudaybiyyah. But he could not unsee the love the companions had for him. When mercy filled Makkah after its conquest, Urwa came to Madinah and embraced Islam, then returned to call his own people. They answered his first call to prayer with arrows, and as he died he asked that no one be harmed for it.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/urwa-ibn-masud

email.subject: The chief who came home to his people
email.alt_subject: He gave the first adhan in Ta'if. They answered with arrows.
email.preview: Urwa ibn Mas'ud had everything his world called success, and he gave it all away.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Urwa ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most noble man of Ta'if. He had standing in two cities, the trust of warring tribes, and a reputation as a peacemaker who once paid off old blood debts from his own pocket just to bring calm to Makkah.

There is an authentic narration in which the Prophet ﷺ describes the prophets he was shown, and says the person who most resembled Isa, the son of Mary, was Urwa.

He first met the Prophet ﷺ as an opponent. Makkah sent him to negotiate at Hudaybiyyah, and he came to push the Muslims around. But he could not unsee what he found there. He watched companions who would not raise their voices before the Prophet ﷺ, who almost competed to serve him, who would never give him up. Urwa had stood in the courts of Caesar, of Kisra, and of the Negus. He went home and told Makkah plainly: I have never seen a people love a leader the way these people love this man.

Years later, when mercy filled Makkah after its conquest, something in him settled. Urwa travelled to Madinah of his own choosing and embraced Islam. Then he asked to go home and call his people to faith. The Prophet ﷺ warned him: they will kill you. Urwa could not believe it. My people love me, he said, so much that if they found me asleep they would not dare wake me.

At dawn he stood on his rooftop and gave the first call to prayer ever raised in Ta'if. His own people answered it with arrows. As he lay dying, his kin spoke of revenge, and he refused. Do not kill anyone for me, he said. Let my blood be a charity. Let there be peace.

There is a quiet lesson in him that is easy to miss. He carried the truth into the hardest room of all, the home that knew him before he changed, and he held no grudge when it was not received.

May Allah be pleased with him, and give us the courage to carry what is true into our own difficult rooms.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Urwa ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most noble man of Ta'if. His father led one of its great houses, his mother came from the nobles of Makkah, and he was respected in both cities at once.

He was a peacemaker by nature. To end a feud, he once paid the blood money owed for old killings out of his own pocket, so that calm could return to a divided city. He travelled widely, brought tribes together, and was known as a man you did not find lying or scheming.

There is an authentic narration in which the Prophet ﷺ described the prophets he had been shown, and said that the one who most resembled Isa, the son of Mary, was Urwa. He was a man of striking and dignified appearance.

He first came to the Prophet ﷺ as an opponent, sent by Makkah to negotiate at Hudaybiyyah. He tried to be harsh. But he stayed long enough to watch the companions, and what he saw undid him. He had stood in the courts of the greatest kings on earth, and he had never seen a people love anyone the way these people loved this man.

When mercy filled Makkah after its conquest, Urwa travelled to Madinah on his own and embraced Islam. Then he asked to go home and call his people to faith. The Prophet ﷺ warned him that they would kill him. Urwa could not believe it. My people love me, he said, so much that they would not dare wake me from sleep.

At dawn he stood on his rooftop in Ta'if and gave the call to prayer, the first ever raised there. His own people answered it with arrows. As he lay dying, he forbade any revenge. Let my blood be a charity, he said. Let there be peace.

When the news reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ likened Urwa to the believing man of Surah Ya-Sin, who called his people to the truth and was killed for it, and who said, once he had crossed over, that if only his people knew how his Lord had forgiven him.
storybook.pullquote: Let my blood be a charity. Let there be peace.
storybook.reflection: Urwa carried the truth into the hardest place there is: the home that knew him before he changed. His people met it with arrows, and still he asked that none of them be harmed. To hold what is true gently, and to wish well even for those who wound us, is a courage worth wanting in an ordinary life.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
